product_description_under_30_words: Two box-play best friends bristle when Shu wants to join their cardboard adventures on Sudden Hill.
ember_verdict: Friend play is getting messier now — a third child can feel like being pushed out. This names jealousy without shaming anyone, and shows friendships can stretch to three. Borrow from the library first; it turns up in nurseries a lot.
rank_rationale: #1 over #2: three-way jealousy and repair is the hardest nearly-three friend moment — this story centres that exact triangle rather than a two-child beach snub.
why_it_fits: Models three-way friendship tension, exclusion and repair — the core 34–36m shift beyond emotion labelling.
caveats: LoveReading lists “5+ readers” as a reading level — Scholastic’s 3–6 shelf mark is the age signal used here.
gift_note: Warm after a messy playdate — not a lecture.
ownership_note: Borrow first — common in nurseries and hand-me-down boxes.
review_quality_note: Strong parent praise for jealousy-and-repair theme; Bookshop.org aggregate corroborates high scores.
preloved_signal_note: Picture books hold up well second-hand.
substitute_if_unavailable: Pip and Posy: The New Friend
evidence_notes: Primary set to LoveReading4Kids after Scholastic UK showed out-of-stock on check date and Bookshop.org returned intermittent 403 on availability fetch; Scholastic age mark captured separately.
buying_factor_memo: Five factors decided order here. First, friend-play specificity: stories where a third child, a snatched toy or a grudge between peers drives the plot beat abstract feelings charts. Second, age overlap: every Top Pick needed a structured publisher or Scholastic age range spanning 34–36 months — not a bare “from 3 years” mark. Third, borrow-first judgement: Pip & Posy and Food Group staples rank high on fit but default to borrow unless the shelf is empty. Fourth, review depth: classics with thousands of UK parent reviews outrank promising 2024 titles with thinner counts (Little Whale stays in Top 5 for freshness but is flagged). Fifth, distinct parent situations: three-way wobbles, jealousy, grudges, sharing rows and mine fights — not five versions of the same sharing lecture.

product_name: Pip and Posy: The New Friend
age_mark_on_listing: Nosy Crow series age band: Ages 2–5
product_description_under_30_words: At the beach, Posy wakes to find Pip playing with new friend Zac — jealousy, then a small kind act reunites them.
ember_verdict: Not another feelings chart — this is the moment your child watches their best friend laugh with someone else. Posy fixes it herself, which gives you language for real playgroup wobbles. Borrow if you already own other Pip & Posy titles.
rank_rationale: #2 over #3: the two-child jealousy beat is more immediately recognisable at playgroup age than Sour Grape’s grudge-loop humour, and Pip & Posy has deeper UK parent recognition.
why_it_fits: Shows jealousy, inclusion and repair in peer play without adult rescue — distinct from 31–33m worry-retelling books.
caveats: Paperback is the value pick; board book ISBN differs.
gift_note: Warm story gift after a playdate bust-up.
ownership_note: High duplication risk — check the shelf and nursery bag first.
review_quality_note: Consistently high scores across Nosy Crow editions.
preloved_signal_note: Ubiquitous second-hand — check for creased covers only.
evidence_notes: Primary switched from Nosy Crow to Amazon UK after Nosy Crow returned 403 on availability fetch while remaining smoke-OK; Nosy Crow retained as alternate.

product_description_under_30_words: A grape who stockpiles grudges learns forgiveness after realising a birthday mix-up — and how sour holding on feels.
ember_verdict: When friend play goes wrong, nearly-threes can stay cross long after the toy is back. This is funny enough for repeat reads and gives you a light way into sorry and moving on. Library copy is fine if you already own The Good Egg.
rank_rationale: #3 over #4: forgiveness after staying cross is a distinct friend-play skill from sharing hardware — and Sour Grape’s review depth beats the newer Little Whale.
why_it_fits: Forgiveness and fairness in friendships — social repair, not basic emotion naming.
caveats: Some wordplay lands above toddler level; the visual story carries it.
gift_note: Funny enough to re-read after a squabble.
ownership_note: Food Group series is widely owned — borrow before buying another.
rating_source: Amazon UK / Goodreads aggregate (UK paperback ISBN 9780063283800)

product_description_under_30_words: Little Whale hates sharing the pod, snacks and Mum’s attention until friend Blue models what sharing looks like.
ember_verdict: Fresh 2024 pick for the tug-of-war over the good toy or the front of the queue. Less abstract than colour jars — a concrete story about turn-taking in group play. Worth buying if your library hold queue is long.
rank_rationale: #4 over #5: group sharing and attention jealousy is a sharper nearly-three turn-taking lesson than a two-character chair fight, and it is the freshest title parents are less likely to already own.
why_it_fits: Sharing and attention jealousy in peer contexts — matches turn-taking cluster without repeating 28–30m emotion-label books.
caveats: Newer title — fewer reviews than classics; strong editorial comparables.
gift_note: Good gift if they do not already own three sharing books.
ownership_note: Lower duplication risk than Pip & Posy — fewer households own it yet.
review_quality_note: Review count above bar but thinner than classics — flagged for founder spot-check.

product_description_under_30_words: Two furry friends battle over one chair, shouting “It’s mine!” until they choose to play together instead.
ember_verdict: The “that’s MINE” phase meets friend play. Short, shouty text mirrors real arguments, then pivots to shared play — handy after playdates where nobody wanted to swap. Borrow if you already have two sharing books on the go.
rank_rationale: #5: the chair fight is the quickest read for mine-shouting rows — kept in Top 5 as the low-friction borrow-first option when you need language tonight, not another fresh purchase.
why_it_fits: Turn-taking and territory fights between peers — practical social rules, not naming happy/sad.
caveats: Hachette product page highlights ebook ISBN; paperback edition cross-checked via publisher age data.
